name: Acceptable Uses of Synthetic Responses in Automated Scoring Baseline
summary: The updated baseline finds that synthetic student responses are most defensible in automated scoring as supporting, diagnostic, or developmental evidence: pre-pilot scorer checks and challenge sets, controlled training augmentation, cold-start training after label audit, human-benchmarked LLM annotation, augmentation calibration, and bounded fairness, robustness, or detection stress tests. A 2026-06-03 update adds direct operational state summative evidence from the New Mexico MSSA technical report: LLM-generated synthetic Writing responses were used as limited training augmentation for sparse high-score examples after scoring-staff review and state approval. This supports tightly governed training augmentation even in consequential settings, but it does not support using synthetic responses alone as final validation, deployment-readiness evidence, subgroup fairness evidence, cut-score evidence, or a substitute for real or independently human-scored response data.
main_blockers: The first pass is targeted rather than systematic, and the 2026-06-03 operational update is a targeted review update rather than a systematic policy review.
Included sources span constructed responses, EFL essays, short-answer content scoring, adaptive writing, simulated essays, and one adjacent admissions SJT context.
The strongest new consequential evidence is one state summative technical report for limited training augmentation, not a broad cross-state, vendor, or accountability-policy norm.
Several strong-sounding augmentation examples rely on real-response validation, human labels, expert scoring, scoring-staff approval, or human-benchmarked model annotation.
The New Mexico source does not fully specify prompt/model details, subgroup effects, cut-score evidence, or validation-set composition relative to approved synthetic rows.
Access-limited candidates such as Ipci et al. are screened but not used as direct support.
best_current_signals: The New Mexico MSSA technical report is the strongest current signal for acceptable operational use: synthetic Writing responses were used as limited training augmentation for sparse high-score examples in a state summative assessment after scoring-staff review and state approval.
Frohn et al. place labeled synthetic responses and challenge sets in a pre-pilot role and still require real student pilot validation.
Yoo et al. use a large synthetic essay subset for AES training while preserving real classroom essays for validation and testing.
Bexte and Zesch show generated target labels require audit or manual relabeling before generated short answers become useful for content-scoring model training.
Nebhi et al. benchmark LLM annotators against expert human ratings before using majority-vote annotation for synthetic writing responses.
Wolfe and Barber use expert scoring, agreement metrics, and realism ratings to calibrate simulated essays for augmentation suitability.

Rezayi et al. provide lower-stakes formative evidence that real-plus-synthetic training can improve communication-skills scoring while preserving real learner responses for evaluation and recommending human review for summative borderline cases.

Djagba et al. provide classroom/in-term evidence that GPT-generated responses can improve rare-category NGSS scoring under held-out real-response evaluation, while explicitly calling for expert checks and additional validity work.
Wang et al. provide adjacent high-stakes evidence for synthetic or constructed off-topic detection sets as validity-protection stress tests, not automated-score validation evidence.
Walsh et al. provide adjacent open-response scoring evidence that synthetic demographic variants can be diagnostic fairness stress-test material, not subgroup validation evidence.
interpretive_note: For this review question, acceptable use depends on workflow role and decision consequence. Synthetic responses can help develop, debug, calibrate, augment, or stress-test an automated scorer when response origin, label source, rubric label space, real-response comparator, and downstream role are explicit. In consequential settings, the current evidence supports only controlled training augmentation or validity-protection stress testing when human review or approval, real-response validation, and monitoring boundaries are explicit. Synthetic responses should not be treated as standalone validation unless the claim is restricted to the synthetic-response distribution and its labels are independently audited.
limitations: This claim does not review automated scoring agreement writ large.

The claim does not establish that synthetic responses can replace real student responses for scorer validation, final deployment readiness, subgroup fairness, cut-score validation, or accountability decisions.
The claim does not validate any specific automated scorer, task, model, prompt strategy, grade band, subject, demographic subgroup, or rubric.
The New Mexico report supports limited operational training augmentation in one state summative context but does not establish a general high-stakes policy or synthetic-only validation pathway.
Walsh et al. is adjacent assessment evidence from an admissions SJT context and is included only for the stress-test use category.

Wang et al. uses mismatched real operational spoken responses to create off-topic examples, so it is adjacent high-stakes detection evidence rather than LLM-generated response or scorer-validation evidence.
Abstract-only or metadata-only sources should not be promoted into direct support without full-text review.
update_notes: A later pass should add full-text Ipci et al. if accessible because it appears directly relevant to automated grading augmentation.
Future synthesis should separate training augmentation, pre-pilot validation, calibration, stress testing, and final validation as distinct use classes.

Future search should look for additional operational technical reports, state documentation, vendor documentation, and policy guidance that separate training augmentation from final validation and live scoring decisions.
Search updates should continue excluding generic human-AI scoring studies unless synthetic responses are part of the scored corpus.

supporting_evidence.label: Pre-pilot scorer checks and challenge sets
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Synthetic responses can support pre-pilot scorer checks, calibration, challenge sets, and item/scoring-criteria revision, but not final validation without real student responses.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Frohn et al. explicitly separate synthetic pre-pilot checks from real-response pilot validation in an LLM constructed-response scoring workflow.
limitations: Framework guidance rather than one empirical validation result.
Bounded to constructed-response LLM scoring.

supporting_evidence.label: Training augmentation with real validation boundary
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Synthetic responses can support automated-scoring training augmentation when validation/test evidence remains real-response based.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Yoo et al. use 40,185 synthetic CASE essays for AES training while preserving real DREsS_New essays for validation and testing.
limitations: CASE corruption is not LLM generation.
Bounded to EFL rubric-based essay scoring.

supporting_evidence.label: Generated-label audit before scorer training
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Prompt-requested synthetic labels should be audited or relabeled before generated responses are used to train content-scoring models.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Bexte and Zesch show as-generated labels can be substantially nonconforming and that manual relabeling changes scorer-training utility.
limitations: One short-answer content-scoring setup.
Manual relabeling adds human labor.

supporting_evidence.label: Human-benchmarked model annotation
supporting_evidence.conclusion: LLM annotation can support synthetic-response pipelines only when annotator quality is benchmarked against expert human labels and label provenance remains explicit.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Nebhi et al. select LLM annotators using Cohen kappa against expert human ratings before majority-vote annotation of synthetic writing responses.
limitations: Compact human gold standard.
Majority-vote model labels are not the same as human scoring all synthetic responses.

supporting_evidence.label: Augmentation calibration
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Expert scoring and realism checks can calibrate simulated essays for augmentation suitability, but this remains weaker than direct validation of a deployed scorer.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Wolfe and Barber evaluate simulated essays with expert scores, agreement metrics, score distributions, and realism ratings.
limitations: Preprint evidence.
Downstream scorer validity still requires real or independently scored validation data.

supporting_evidence.label: Diagnostic fairness stress testing
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Synthetic variants can be useful as diagnostic stress tests for scoring behavior, but not as subgroup fairness validation by themselves.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Walsh et al. compare automated evaluations of original Casper responses with synthetic demographic analogs and explicitly leave larger-sample fairness work open.
limitations: Adjacent admissions SJT setting.
Early fairness pilot with a small synthetic-analog sample.

supporting_evidence.label: Controlled operational training augmentation
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Synthetic responses can be acceptable as limited operational training augmentation in a consequential assessment when they address sparse training examples and are governed by documented review, approval, and real-response validation boundaries.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The New Mexico MSSA technical report states that English non-accommodated operational Writing forms used LLM-generated synthetic responses to supplement field-test and operational responses for items with sparse high-score examples, with scoring-staff review and state approval before use.
limitations: One state summative technical report.
Supports training augmentation rather than synthetic-only final validation.
Prompt/model details, subgroup effects, cut-score evidence, and validation-set composition relative to approved synthetic rows are not fully specified in the extracted record.

supporting_evidence.label: Formative hybrid augmentation
supporting_evidence.conclusion: In lower-stakes formative scoring, real-plus-synthetic training augmentation can be acceptable when evaluation remains based on real learner responses and summative or borderline decisions retain human review.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Rezayi et al. evaluate Qwen-generated synthetic learner responses for communication-skills scoring, report better real-response F1 for a real-plus-synthetic training condition than real-only training at the same sparse real-data level, and frame summative use around hybrid human review.
limitations: Formative professional-education setting.
Real annotated learner responses remain necessary.
Summative or borderline cases are not delegated wholly to synthetic-response-trained automation.

supporting_evidence.label: Classroom rare-category augmentation
supporting_evidence.conclusion: For classroom or in-term research uses, synthetic responses can support rare-category training augmentation when held-out evaluation remains real-response based and additional validity work is treated as required.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Djagba et al. report that GPT-generated synthetic responses improved rare-category NGSS scoring performance on held-out real student responses, while also calling for expert quality checks and further validity work.
limitations: Classroom/in-term research context rather than grade-bearing operational decision evidence.
Conference preprint evidence.
The source itself calls for additional validity work and expert quality checks.

supporting_evidence.label: High-stakes detection and validity-protection stress testing
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Synthetic or constructed response sets can support high-stakes detection and validity-protection stress tests, but this is distinct from validating automated scoring of on-topic student responses.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Wang et al. use operational spoken responses and mismatched responses from other prompts to construct off-topic cases for a high-stakes English speaking assessment detector, improving detection metrics without making a scorer-validation claim.
limitations: Synthetic cases are constructed from mismatched real operational responses rather than LLM-generated responses.
The task is off-topic detection and validity protection, not automated scoring validation.
Does not establish subgroup fairness, cut-score, or live scoring decision evidence.

applies_when: Synthetic responses are used as supporting, diagnostic, developmental, calibration, augmentation, or stress-test evidence.
Response origin, label source, rubric label space, real-response comparator, and workflow role are explicit.
Operational use is limited to controlled training augmentation or detection/stress testing with documented human review or approval, real-response validation, and monitoring boundaries.
does_not_apply_when: Synthetic responses are used as the sole basis for final validation, deployment readiness, subgroup fairness, cut scores, or operational high-stakes decisions.
A single operational training-augmentation example is generalized into a broad vendor, state, or accountability policy without additional direct evidence.
unknown_or_unreviewed_contexts: Specific grade bands, subjects, demographic subgroups, operational scoring vendors, and local policy thresholds remain unreviewed unless separately sourced.
Additional state summative programs, certification/licensure uses, placement or promotion decisions, and grade-bearing classroom deployments need separate direct review.
